seoTitle: AcelaMicro Solutions | Industrial Sensor & BMS Applications | LiTong
seoDescription: Explore AcelaMicro application solutions including industrial sensor interfaces and automotive battery management systems with complete reference designs.

faqs.question: What application solutions does AcelaMicro offer?
faqs.answer: AcelaMicro provides comprehensive application solutions for industrial and automotive markets. Our industrial sensor interface solution enables high-precision measurement of strain gauges, thermocouples, RTDs, and pressure sensors with 24-bit resolution and excellent noise immunity. The automotive battery management system solution supports EV and energy storage applications with ASIL-D capable architecture, high-voltage isolation, and precise cell monitoring. Both solutions include complete reference designs, BOM lists, and software examples to accelerate your development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our solutions below to find the right application for your project, or contact our FAE team for customized solution recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I select the right AcelaMicro solution for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right solution depends on your application requirements. For industrial measurement applications requiring high-precision sensor interfaces, choose our Industrial Sensor Interface Solution featuring the ACM2420 24-bit ADC and ACM8221 instrumentation amplifier. For electric vehicle or energy storage applications requiring battery management, select our Automotive BMS Solution with ACM1604 ADCs and ACM1050 isolated transceivers. Consider factors like measurement accuracy, channel count, communication requirements, and safety certifications when making your selection. Our FAE team can provide detailed technical guidance to ensure optimal solution selection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review solution specifications and key components below, or request a consultation with our FAE team for personalized recommendations.

faqs.question: What is included in AcelaMicro's solution offerings?
faqs.answer: Each AcelaMicro solution includes a complete reference design package to accelerate your development. This includes detailed system architecture, complete BOM with AcelaMicro components, technical specifications, core advantages analysis, customer case studies demonstrating real-world applications, and comprehensive FAE insights with best practices and common pitfalls. Solutions also include software examples, PCB layout guidelines, and application notes. The reference designs are validated and ready for customization to your specific requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Explore the detailed solution pages below to access complete reference designs and technical documentation.

faqs.question: Can AcelaMicro solutions be customized for specific requirements?
faqs.answer: Yes, AcelaMicro solutions can be customized to meet your specific application requirements. The modular architecture of our solutions allows for flexible configuration of channel counts, sensor types, and communication interfaces. Our engineering team can support customization of hardware designs, firmware modifications, and integration with your existing systems. For high-volume applications, we can also discuss custom IC modifications or special testing requirements. Contact our FAE team early in your design phase to discuss customization options and ensure optimal solution fit.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team to discuss your specific requirements and customization options for your application.

faqs.question: What support is available for implementing AcelaMicro solutions?
faqs.answer: LiTong Electronics provides comprehensive support for implementing AcelaMicro solutions. Our FAE team offers application guidance, design review services, and debugging assistance throughout your development process. We provide evaluation boards and reference designs for hands-on testing. Technical documentation includes application notes, PCB layout guidelines, and software examples. For complex implementations, we can arrange direct support from AcelaMicro's engineering team. Post-implementation support includes troubleshooting assistance and optimization recommendations to ensure your solution meets performance targets.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team to discuss your implementation needs and access evaluation resources.

solutions.title: Industrial Sensor Interface Solution
solutions.name: Industrial Sensor Interface Solution
solutions.description: Complete signal conditioning and acquisition solution for industrial sensors including strain gauges, thermocouples, RTDs, and pressure sensors. Features high-precision measurement, noise immunity, and flexible configuration options.
solutions.longDescription: The Industrial Sensor Interface Solution from AcelaMicro provides a complete signal chain for precision measurement of industrial sensors. This solution integrates the ACM2420 24-bit sigma-delta ADC, ACM8221 instrumentation amplifier, and ACM5320 RTD signal conditioner to support multiple sensor types with exceptional accuracy. The ratiometric measurement architecture eliminates reference voltage drift, while 120 dB CMRR ensures reliable operation in noisy industrial environments. With isolated RS-485 communication and wide operating temperature range, this solution is ideal for process control, quality assurance, and industrial automation applications.
benefits: 24-bit precision measurement with 20 effective bits of resolution
Multi-sensor support reduces system complexity and inventory
120 dB CMRR eliminates noise in industrial environments
Modbus RTU protocol enables easy PLC integration
Complete reference design accelerates time to market
features: Multi-sensor support: strain gauges, thermocouples, RTDs, pressure sensors
Advanced noise filtering and 50/60 Hz rejection
Compact modular design for easy integration

keyComponents.description: 24-bit sigma-delta ADC with integrated PGA for high-resolution sensor measurement
Precision instrumentation amplifier for bridge sensor signal conditioning
RTD signal conditioner with excitation and linearization
RS-485 transceiver for industrial network communication
Low-noise LDO for clean analog power supply

coreAdvantages.title: High Precision Measurement
coreAdvantages.description: 24-bit ADC with 20 effective bits of resolution enables detection of microvolt-level signals, providing measurement accuracy of +/- 0.01% full scale for demanding industrial applications.

coreAdvantages.title: Multi-Sensor Flexibility
coreAdvantages.description: Single platform supports strain gauges, thermocouples, RTDs, and voltage output sensors, reducing inventory complexity and enabling standardized system designs.

coreAdvantages.title: Industrial Noise Immunity
coreAdvantages.description: 120 dB CMRR, 50/60 Hz rejection, and isolated communication ensure reliable operation in electrically noisy industrial environments with motor drives and switching equipment.

coreAdvantages.title: Ratiometric Measurement
coreAdvantages.description: Ratiometric architecture eliminates errors from excitation voltage drift, providing stable long-term accuracy without frequent calibration.

coreAdvantages.title: Easy Integration
coreAdvantages.description: Standard Modbus RTU protocol and compact form factor enable quick integration with existing PLC and SCADA systems.

customerCases.challenge: The customer needed to monitor force in 16 assembly stations simultaneously with 0.1% accuracy for quality control. Existing systems suffered from noise pickup in the long cable runs and drift over time requiring frequent calibration.
customerCases.solution: Implemented AcelaMicro's industrial sensor interface solution with ACM2420 ADCs and ACM8221 instrumentation amplifiers. The 120 dB CMRR eliminated noise issues, while ratiometric measurement provided long-term stability. RS-485 communication enabled reliable data transmission over 100m cable runs.
customerCases.result: Achieved +/- 0.05% measurement accuracy, exceeding the 0.1% requirement. Calibration interval extended from weekly to semi-annually, reducing maintenance costs by 80%. System downtime due to measurement errors eliminated completely.

customerCases.challenge: A chemical plant required temperature monitoring at 32 points throughout the facility with SIL2 safety integrity level. The environment had severe electrical noise from variable frequency drives and required intrinsically safe interfaces for hazardous areas.
customerCases.solution: Deployed isolated sensor interface modules using ACM5320 RTD conditioners with ACM1050 isolated CAN transceivers. The 5000Vrms isolation met safety requirements, while the 50/60 Hz rejection filtered power line interference. Dual-redundant design achieved SIL2 rating.
customerCases.result: System achieved SIL2 certification with zero safety incidents over 2 years of operation. Temperature measurement accuracy of +/- 0.1°C enabled tighter process control, improving product yield by 3.5%.

faeInsights.content: The key to successful industrial sensor interface design is understanding the complete signal chain from sensor to software. Many designers focus only on the ADC resolution, but the analog front-end is equally critical. The instrumentation amplifier's CMRR determines noise rejection capability, while proper PCB layout prevents ground loops and EMI pickup. I've seen systems with 24-bit ADCs achieve only 12-bit effective resolution due to poor analog design. AcelaMicro's integrated approach with the ACM2420 and ACM8221 addresses these issues by providing matched components with optimized specifications. For thermocouple applications, always use differential inputs and keep the cold junction compensation sensor close to the connection terminals. For strain gauges, ratiometric measurement is essential - don't use a fixed voltage reference. The most common mistake I see is insufficient power supply decoupling; use multiple capacitor values (100nF, 1uF, 10uF) to cover different frequency ranges.
keyTakeaways: Analog front-end design is as important as ADC selection
Use ratiometric measurement for resistive sensors to eliminate reference drift
Differential inputs and proper grounding are essential for noise immunity
Match capacitor values to frequency ranges for optimal decoupling
Test with actual sensors in the target environment

decisionFramework.title: Sensor Interface Design Decision Framework
steps: Identify sensor type and output characteristics
Determine noise requirements based on measurement resolution
Design PCB with proper grounding and shielding
considerations: For multi-channel systems, consider channel-to-channel isolation requirements
For safety-critical applications, evaluate need for redundant channels and diagnostics
Always prototype and test with actual sensors in the real environment before finalizing the design

faqs.question: What types of sensors can this solution interface with?
faqs.answer: This industrial sensor interface solution supports multiple sensor types including strain gauges and load cells (using ACM8221 instrumentation amplifiers), thermocouples (types K, J, T, E, N, S, R, B with cold junction compensation), RTDs (Pt100, Pt1000 with 2, 3, or 4-wire configurations using ACM5320), and voltage-output sensors (0-10V, +/-10V). The flexible architecture allows mixing different sensor types on the same platform. Each input channel can be independently configured for the specific sensor type through software. The 24-bit ADC provides sufficient resolution for all common industrial sensors, while the programmable gain amplifiers optimize signal levels for each sensor's output range.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team with your specific sensor requirements for a customized channel configuration recommendation.

faqs.question: How do I minimize electrical noise in long cable runs?
faqs.answer: Minimizing noise in long cable runs requires a multi-layered approach. First, use shielded twisted-pair cable for all sensor connections - the twisting reduces magnetic interference while the shield blocks electric fields. Connect the shield to ground at the receiver end only to avoid ground loops. Second, implement differential signal transmission using the instrumentation amplifier's differential inputs - this rejects common-mode noise picked up equally on both wires. Third, add RC filters (1kΩ + 100nF) at the amplifier inputs to limit bandwidth and remove high-frequency noise. Fourth, use the ACM2420's 50/60 Hz rejection feature to eliminate power line interference. Fifth, ensure proper grounding - use a star ground configuration with analog and digital grounds connected at a single point. For extremely noisy environments, consider the isolated version using ACM1050 transceivers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use shielded twisted-pair cable, differential inputs, input filtering, and proper grounding for best noise immunity.

faqs.question: What is the recommended calibration procedure?
faqs.answer: The recommended calibration procedure involves three steps: zero calibration, span calibration, and linearity verification. For zero calibration, disconnect the sensor or apply zero input (shorted inputs for voltage measurements, 0°C reference for thermocouples), then record the offset and subtract it from subsequent readings. For span calibration, apply a known full-scale input (precision voltage source or calibrated weight for load cells) and adjust the gain coefficient to match the expected output. For RTDs, use precision resistance standards. For linearity verification, apply inputs at 25%, 50%, and 75% of full scale and verify errors are within specification. The ratiometric measurement architecture minimizes drift, but I recommend recalibration every 6-12 months for critical applications. The system supports both manual calibration through software commands and automated calibration sequences.
faqs.decisionGuide: Perform zero and span calibration at commissioning. Verify linearity at multiple points. Recalibrate every 6-12 months.

faqs.question: Can this solution achieve SIL2 or SIL3 safety ratings?
faqs.answer: The standard configuration can achieve SIL2 with appropriate architectural measures including dual-redundant channels, diagnostic coverage, and proof testing. For SIL2, implement dual-channel architecture with comparison diagnostics - if the two channels disagree beyond a threshold, the system enters a safe state. Include diagnostic checks for power supply monitoring, communication integrity, and ADC self-tests. For SIL3, additional measures are required including diverse technology (using different ADC architectures), increased diagnostic coverage (>99%), and more frequent proof testing. AcelaMicro can provide reference designs and safety manuals for SIL-rated implementations. Contact our FAE team for detailed safety architecture recommendations and documentation support.
faqs.decisionGuide: SIL2 achievable with dual channels and diagnostics. SIL3 requires diverse technology and higher diagnostic coverage. Contact FAE for safety documentation.

faqs.question: What communication protocols are supported?
faqs.answer: The standard solution implements Modbus RTU protocol over RS-485, which is the most widely used industrial communication standard. Modbus RTU provides reliable master-slave communication with CRC error checking. The system supports function codes 03 (read holding registers), 04 (read input registers), 06 (write single register), and 16 (write multiple registers). Each sensor channel maps to specific register addresses for easy PLC integration. For CAN-based systems, the ACM1050 isolated CAN transceiver supports standard CAN 2.0B protocol with device-specific CANopen or J1939 protocol stacks available. Custom protocols can be implemented on the STM32F407 microcontroller. Ethernet connectivity can be added using external Ethernet modules for integration with modern IIoT systems.
faqs.decisionGuide: Modbus RTU over RS-485 is standard. CANopen and Ethernet options available. Custom protocols supported on request.

solutions.title: Automotive Battery Management System
solutions.name: Automotive Battery Management System
solutions.description: High-precision battery management solution for electric vehicles and energy storage systems. Features cell voltage monitoring, temperature sensing, current measurement, and isolated communication for safe and efficient battery operation.
solutions.longDescription: The Automotive Battery Management System (BMS) Solution from AcelaMicro provides comprehensive battery monitoring and management for electric vehicles and energy storage applications. This solution leverages the ACM1604 16-bit precision ADC for accurate cell voltage measurement, ACM1050 isolated CAN transceiver for high-voltage safety isolation, and ACM5320 RTD conditioner for temperature monitoring. With ASIL-D capable architecture, 5000Vrms isolation, and support for multiple battery chemistries, this solution meets the stringent requirements of modern EV and grid storage systems. The modular design scales from small 12-cell packs to large grid-scale installations.
benefits: +/- 1mV cell voltage accuracy maximizes usable battery capacity
5000Vrms isolation ensures safety in high-voltage applications
ASIL-D capable architecture meets automotive functional safety requirements
Supports multiple battery chemistries including Li-ion, LFP, and solid-state
5 Mbps CAN FD enables real-time monitoring and control
Modular architecture scales from 12 to 3000+ cells
features: Supports Li-ion, LiFePO4, and solid-state battery chemistries
Integrated temperature monitoring per cell group
CAN FD communication for real-time monitoring

keyComponents.description: 16-bit ADC for cell voltage and current measurement
Isolated CAN transceiver for high-voltage isolation
Buck converter for auxiliary power supply
Low-noise LDO for precision analog circuits
RTD conditioner for cell temperature monitoring

coreAdvantages.title: High-Precision Cell Monitoring
coreAdvantages.description: +/- 1mV voltage accuracy and +/- 0.5°C temperature accuracy enable precise state-of-charge and state-of-health calculations, maximizing battery performance and lifetime.

coreAdvantages.title: High-Voltage Safety Isolation
coreAdvantages.description: 5000Vrms isolation with 50 kV/μs CMTI ensures safe operation in high-voltage battery packs up to 1000V, protecting low-voltage control systems.

coreAdvantages.title: Automotive Safety Integrity
coreAdvantages.description: ASIL-D capable architecture with redundant measurements, comprehensive diagnostics, and fail-safe operation meets the highest automotive functional safety requirements.

coreAdvantages.title: Flexible Cell Chemistry Support
coreAdvantages.description: Configurable for Li-ion, LiFePO4, and emerging solid-state chemistries with adjustable voltage thresholds and balancing parameters.

coreAdvantages.title: Fast CAN FD Communication
coreAdvantages.description: 5 Mbps CAN FD enables real-time monitoring and control with low latency for active safety systems and fast fault response.

customerCases.challenge: A leading EV manufacturer needed a BMS for an 800V, 100kWh battery pack with ASIL-D safety rating. The system required monitoring 192 cells with 1mV accuracy, fast fault response, and operation in harsh automotive environments with severe EMC.
customerCases.solution: Implemented AcelaMicro's BMS solution using ACM1604 ADCs for precise cell monitoring and ACM1050 isolated CAN transceivers for safe high-voltage communication. The 5000Vrms isolation met safety requirements, while the 50 kV/μs CMTI ensured reliable communication during switching transients.
customerCases.result: Achieved ASIL-D certification with comprehensive fault coverage. Cell voltage accuracy of +/- 0.8mV exceeded the +/- 1mV target. System operated reliably in EMC tests with 200V/m field strength. Battery pack efficiency improved by 2% due to precise SOC calculation.

customerCases.challenge: An energy storage company required a BMS for a 1MWh grid-scale battery system with 10-year lifetime requirement. The system needed to monitor 3,072 cells across multiple racks with centralized monitoring and predictive maintenance capabilities.
customerCases.solution: Deployed modular BMS architecture using AcelaMicro's solution with distributed measurement modules communicating via isolated CAN bus to a central controller. The high-precision measurement enabled accurate SOH tracking for predictive maintenance. Redundant communication paths ensured system availability.
customerCases.result: System achieved 99.99% availability over 2 years of operation. Predictive maintenance algorithms identified cell degradation 3 months before failure, enabling proactive replacement. Battery capacity retention improved by 15% over 5 years compared to previous BMS design.

faeInsights.content: Battery management system design requires balancing precision, safety, and cost. The most critical aspect is accurate cell voltage measurement - even 5mV of error can significantly impact SOC estimation and usable capacity. I've seen systems with poor measurement accuracy leave 10-15% of battery capacity unused due to conservative safety margins. AcelaMicro's ACM1604 with its 1 LSB INL specification provides the precision needed for accurate SOC/SOH calculations. For safety, the isolation barrier is critical - the ACM1050's 5000Vrms rating provides margin for 800V battery packs. The CMTI specification is equally important; I've debugged systems where communication errors occurred during motor switching transients due to insufficient CMTI. For ASIL-D, redundancy is essential - implement dual ADC channels with comparison, redundant temperature sensors, and watchdog timers. The most common BMS failure mode I've encountered is cell balancing FET failure - implement diagnostic current measurements to detect stuck-on or open FETs.
keyTakeaways: Voltage measurement accuracy directly impacts usable battery capacity
Isolation rating and CMTI are both critical for high-voltage safety
ASIL-D requires redundancy and comprehensive diagnostics
Cell balancing FET diagnostics prevent safety hazards
EMC validation must include representative switching transients

decisionFramework.title: BMS Design Decision Framework
steps: Determine cell count and pack voltage for architecture
Calculate required measurement accuracy based on SOC precision targets
Design isolation strategy meeting safety requirements
Implement ASIL-rated architecture with appropriate diagnostics
Design thermal management for cell balancing power dissipation
Validate EMC performance in representative environment
considerations: For high-voltage packs (>400V), use distributed BMS architecture with module-level monitoring and centralized control
Always implement comprehensive fault detection including open-wire detection, overvoltage/undervoltage monitoring, and communication integrity checks

faqs.question: What battery chemistries are supported by this BMS solution?
faqs.answer: The AcelaMicro BMS solution supports multiple lithium-based battery chemistries including NMC (NCM) Li-ion with nominal cell voltage of 3.6-3.7V, LFP (LiFePO4) with nominal 3.2V, NCA with nominal 3.6V, and LTO with nominal 2.3V. The system is also compatible with emerging solid-state battery technologies. The configurable voltage thresholds for overvoltage, undervoltage, and balancing can be adjusted through software to match the specific chemistry's characteristics. The measurement range of 1.5V to 5V covers all common lithium chemistries. For other chemistries like sodium-ion or flow batteries, contact our FAE team to verify compatibility and required modifications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Configure voltage thresholds based on your specific battery chemistry. Contact FAE for non-standard chemistries.

faqs.question: How does the cell balancing work and how fast is it?
faqs.answer: The BMS implements passive cell balancing using resistive discharge. When a cell's voltage exceeds the pack average by a configurable threshold (typically 10-50mV), the balancing FET for that cell turns on, connecting a resistor across the cell to dissipate excess charge. The standard balancing current is 200mA, which can balance a 100Ah pack with 100mV imbalance in approximately 2 hours during charging. For faster balancing, multiple modules can be paralleled or external active balancing circuits can be added. Balancing typically occurs during charging when excess energy is available, but can also be performed during idle periods. The balancing resistors are sized for continuous operation and include thermal monitoring to prevent overheating.
faqs.decisionGuide: 200mA balancing suitable for most applications. Contact FAE for active balancing options if faster balancing required.

faqs.question: What safety certifications does this BMS solution have?
faqs.answer: The AcelaMicro BMS solution is designed to support ASIL-D functional safety rating per ISO 26262 when implemented with the recommended architecture including redundant measurements and comprehensive diagnostics. The hardware components including ACM1050 isolated transceivers are AEC-Q100 qualified for automotive applications. The 5000Vrms isolation meets IEC 60664-1 requirements for reinforced insulation in high-voltage systems. For UL certification of the complete battery pack, additional system-level testing is required. AcelaMicro can provide safety manuals, FMEDA analysis, and technical support for achieving safety certifications. Contact our FAE team for detailed safety documentation and certification support.
faqs.decisionGuide: ASIL-D capable with proper architecture. AEC-Q100 qualified components. Contact FAE for safety documentation.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the state of charge (SOC) accurately?
faqs.answer: Accurate SOC calculation requires combining multiple measurement techniques. The Coulomb counting method integrates current over time to track charge flow, but suffers from drift due to measurement errors. The voltage-based method uses the open-circuit voltage vs SOC relationship, but is only accurate when the battery has rested. The recommended approach is an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) that combines voltage, current, and temperature measurements with a battery model. AcelaMicro's BMS reference design includes EKF-based SOC estimation achieving +/- 3% accuracy. For best results, calibrate the battery model parameters for your specific cell type. Temperature compensation is essential as cell characteristics vary significantly with temperature. Implement periodic recalibration at known SOC points (like full charge) to correct drift.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use EKF combining voltage, current, and temperature. Calibrate for specific cell type. Implement periodic recalibration.

faqs.question: Can this BMS solution scale to large battery packs?
faqs.answer: Yes, the modular architecture scales from small 12-cell packs to large grid-scale systems with thousands of cells. Each ACM1604-based module monitors up to 16 cells, and modules can be daisy-chained via isolated CAN bus. For a typical EV pack with 96 cells, use 6 modules. For grid storage with 3,000+ cells, use 200+ modules connected to a central controller. The CAN FD communication at 5 Mbps supports update rates of 100ms for all cells even in large systems. For very large installations, implement hierarchical architecture with local controllers aggregating data from multiple modules. The system supports automatic module discovery and addressing, simplifying installation and maintenance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Scales from 12 to 3000+ cells. Use hierarchical architecture for very large systems. Automatic module discovery supported.
